caption.text: Before the creation of Cathedral Homes, one of the first public housing developments in Boston, stood the type of lower income community that characterized the South End during the first half of the 20th century. The buildings that occupied this area largely consisted of brick houses that were used as either boarding, tenement, or settlement housing.
caption.title: Start

caption.text: One set of buildings that encapsulate the type of housing that existed at Cathedral Homes are the row brick buildings on 13-37 East Canton street. These are excellent examples of the type of crowded and unkept living conditions that were found in the heart of the area now known as Cathedral Home.
caption.title: 13-37 East Canton Street

caption.text: From the front these buildings give a cleaner appearance, but once the context that these homes were tenement housing is revealed, the space seems far more cramped considering the amount of people that lived in each of these buildings.
caption.title: Tenement houses, 35-37 East Canton Street in 1922

caption.text: The back of these buildings reveals a clearer picture of the poor upkeep and crowded nature of these tenement houses.
caption.title: Tenement houses, Rear Exterior, 35-37 East Canton Street

caption.text: While the appearances of the tenement houses in the heart of what is now Cathedral Homes presents a rougher and less appealing urban environment, residences that stood on the periphery of the residential area along East Brookline Street and in front of Franklin Park retained more architectural features like the entrance stoops and iron hand rails. These buildings recall back to the time before many of the wealthier Boston residents [left the South End]( https://sah-archipedia.org/essays/MA-01-0001-0009) to move to the newly created Back Bay neighborhood in the 1870s.
caption.title: Houses on the Periphery of the Community as seen on the 1917 Atlas

caption.text: This picture was shared on the Vintage Boston Facebook Group in which a member is sharing a picture of his grandfather and great-uncle in 1922 standing in front of a house in the South End. The contributor mentions that his grandfather lived at 22 East Brookline Street in 1922, the contributor does mistakenly refer to this part of Boston as Roxbury rather than the South End. The characteristic architecture of the building in the back does have the characteristic features of a South End House. Nevertheless, there is little primary source material that could be found which corroborates that this was indeed the address the contributor’s residences lived in, with his last name having no record in the Boston directories of 1922.
caption.title: Typical South End Brick Residence Home that is Presumably on East Brookline Street

caption.text: Additionally, the houses on Atlascope on 22 East Brookline Street do appear to have that entrance stoop as seen in the picture. Therefore, there is a good chance that the building in the back is a now demolished residential home that stood in Cathedral Homes that could even be the 22 East Brookline residence.
caption.title: Evidence of the Stoop seen on a 1922 Atlas

caption.text: To further understand the community that existed before Cathedral Homes, one must look at the importance of religion that played in the lives of the residents. In fact, Cathedral Homes gets its name from the neighboring Cathedral of Holy Cross which provided an anchor to the predominantly catholic community in the area. Many of the residents in the community also tended to be immigrants or the descendants of immigrants from predominantly Catholic countries like Ireland and Italy. Therefore, the Catholic church ended provided a center of worship, community, and education through the catholic school it hosted next to the church.
caption.title: Religion in the Community

caption.text: The Cathedral of Holy Cross is the largest Catholic church in New England. It is of the early English Gothic style, and it reflects greatly on the large catholic population in the South End that continues to exist today.
caption.title: View of Cathedral of Holy Cross from Washington Street in the 1920s

caption.text: To recap, the community at Cathedral Homes before redevelopment was predominantly catholic and lower income. Many of the people who lived here had to find ways to balance long working hours, taking care of their families, and at times needed access to other forms of social services.
caption.title: Work and Social Services

caption.text: While living conditions were poor in many of the boarding and tenement houses that existed, many of the residents took advantage of the neighboring factories, businesses, and mills for employment opportunities.
caption.title: Proximity to Employment Opportunities

caption.text: Lumber Yards and other woodworking industries populated in the areas near Cathedral Homes. This form of industry started thrived in the South End, especially near the waterfront via the [Fort Point Channel, South Bay, and Roxbury Canal]( https://sah-archipedia.org/essays/MA-01-0001-0009-0001).
caption.title: A Popular Industry: Lumber Yards

caption.text: While not exactly a lumber yard, Perkins Wood Working Co, which was located at 38 Wareham Street, is an example of the many wood-working companies that would have employed nearby residents and worked with the surrounding lumber yards.
caption.title: Perkins Wood Working Co Advertisement in 1916 Boston Directory

caption.text: Most industry in this area started to dissipate once more, the waterfront was filled in midway through the 20th century. However, by 1938 there was evidence that the lumber industry had started to shrink. Two of the lumber yards that were next to St. Helena’s had been bought and incorporated. While the lumber yards and woodworking shops like Perkins were gone.
caption.title: Lumber Yards Disappearing in the 1938 Atlas

caption.text: That is not to say that all lumber mills and woodworking shops were gone by 1938.
caption.title: Surviving Lumber Industry in 1938 Atlas

caption.text: While the industries were close to the Cathedral Homes area community, long working hours and low pay made it hard for many in the community to make ends meet. At the time there was a lack of social safety nets offered by the government, and as a result many in the community had to turn to private organizations for assistance.
caption.title: Social Services

caption.text: The South End House Association and South Bay Union: For low-income immigrant communities in the South End, assimilation and balancing home responsibilities was a challenge. The South End House Association and South Bay Union attempted to provide immigrants and low-income households services such as helping preserve family life, promote cultural and intellectual development, provide employment counseling, daycare, and tutoring in the English language. This building, while not situated in the Cathedral Homes area, primarily served nearby residents.
caption.title: The South End House Association and South Bay Union

caption.text: Within the Cathedral Homes area, South End House Association established two single and working women residences that offered cheap housing opportunities. The women residences helped balance responsibilities thanks to the services offered.
caption.title: 43-45 East Canton Women’s Residences

caption.text: Unfortunately, by 1922 the women's South End settlement houses ceased to exist in the community.
caption.title: South End House Association’s Women's Settlement Houses close at the Cathedral Homes area.

caption.text: Although the women’s settlement houses may have left, the South End House Association and the South Bay Union Continued to operate well into the last atlas before the removal of the community in the Cathedral Homes area.
caption.title: South End House Association Still Exists in the 1938 Atlas

caption.text: In addition to the Settlement Houses in the area, religious institutions like the Salvation Army provided social services in the area. Although not reflective of the Catholic community that lived in the area, Salvation Army operated the People’s Palace Hotel.
caption.title: The Salvation Army’s People’s Palace Hotel

caption.text: Within this article, it details the early plans that the Salvation Army had for the construction of the hotel at Washington and Brookline Street. The building did not function just as a normal hotel, rather its purpose is both to serve as cheap hotel for workingmen, and as one of the Salvation Army’s headquarters in Boston. Additionally, the hotel wants to offer its occupants plentiful meeting space and access to cheap resources that would help them avoid living on the streets.
caption.title: Boston Daily Globe 1905 Article on the Salvation Army’s Site Purchase for the People’s Hotel

caption.text: People’s Palace Hotel in 1909: Here is a picture of the People’s Palace Hotel when it was first built. At the time the architectural designed was described as being [“modern in every particular, and as nearly fireproof as possible.”]( https://www.proquest.com/docview/500397722/D9E062822D8847C6PQ/1?accountid=12826&sourcetype=Newspapers) The design also took inspiration from a similar Salvation Army hotel that was built in Cleveland.
caption.title: People’s Palace Hotel in 1909

caption.text: The article here shows that almost three decades after it was first built, the hotel continued to play an active role in helping working men and people around the community around the community. In this case, the hotel shields thousands from extreme colds on December 30, 1933.  A fun detail about this article is that they mixed up the street names of where the hotel was located.
caption.title: Daily Boston Globe 1933 Article Hotel Providing Shelter

caption.text: The Salvation Army continues to house its headquarters right next to where the original structure stood, today. The original building was demolished along with the rest of the buildings to make way for the construction of Cathedral Homes Public Housing Project.
caption.title: The Salvation Army Headquarters in Cathedral Homes in 2026

caption.text: Having moved here at some point between 1917 and 1922, the Catholic Ladies Club represents a Catholic religious organization that operated Settlement Houses within the Cathedral Homes area. The club was located at 18 East Brookline Street, just one house apart from the suspected home of the Vintage Boston Facebook Contributor's 1922 home.
caption.title: Catholic Ladies Club East Brookline Street Settlement House in 1922 Atlas

caption.text: Although this was before the club moved into the Cathedral Homes area, the article covers what type of services the club offered in the South End. This includes teaching young girls various important trades that could be used in the workforce.
caption.title: Boston Daily Globe 1916 Article on the Ladies Catholic Club

caption.text: In this article, the Ladies' Catholic Club is noted for hosting a "Irish Night" at the East Brookline Settlement House. Here, the club provided an illustrated lecture on Ireland. Thus, showing the club is providing one of the many social services that settlement houses tended to provide in the South End. In this case they are both educating and promoting cultural development to its beneficiaries.
caption.title: Boston Daily Globe 1927 Article on Ladies' Catholic Club "Irish Night."
